children.name: Rallus
children.description: Water rails — secretive, laterally compressed marsh birds built to slip silently through dense reedbed. The water rail (vattenrall) is far more often heard than seen: its extraordinary calls range from a squealing piglet-like shriek to a rhythmic rhythmic grunting that echoes across Swedish marshes on still evenings. Despite their skulking habits, water rails are present in virtually every Swedish reedbed of any size, from the Baltic coast to inland lakes around Stockholm. Their laterally flattened bodies — the origin of the expression 'thin as a rail' — let them move at speed through tangled reed stems inaccessible to larger predators. In hard winters when Swedish reedbeds freeze over, water rails sometimes starve; in milder years they are sedentary and can be found year-round.

children.name: Rallus aquaticus
children.description: The vattenrall — one of Sweden's most heard but rarely seen birds. Its pig-like squealing calls ('sharming') carry far across reedbed at dawn and dusk. Found in reedbeds across Sweden from Skåne to Uppland; sedentary where wetlands remain ice-free in winter. Its long red bill probes soft mud for invertebrates, small fish, and plant material. A patient observer sitting quietly at a reedbed edge may eventually watch one emerge to feed at the margin.

children.name: Gallinula
children.description: Moorhens — medium-sized rallids that occupy the interface between open water and emergent vegetation, making them far more visible than most family members. The common moorhen (rörhöna) is a ubiquitous bird on Swedish ponds, lakes, and slow rivers, recognisable by its jerky head-bobbing walk and distinctive red-and-yellow bill shield. Unlike coots, moorhens have lobed rather than fully webbed feet, which are used to grip reed stems as they clamber through vegetation. Moorhens are surprisingly aggressive during the breeding season, chasing rivals with vigorous foot-fights on the water surface. They build floating nests anchored to reeds and are among the earliest breeders in Swedish wetlands, sometimes nesting in late April while ice still lingers at the margins.

children.name: Gallinula chloropus
children.description: The rörhöna — a familiar sight on every Swedish pond and urban waterway, including Stockholm's city parks. Its bright red frontal shield and yellow-tipped bill are unmistakeable. Young birds from earlier broods help their parents raise later clutches — one of the few documented cases of cooperative breeding in Swedish waterbirds. Partial migrant in Sweden; most depart southward in autumn but some winter in mild lowland areas.

children.name: Fulica
children.description: Coots — robust, slate-black rallids with distinctive white bills and frontal shields that are familiar and often dominant birds on open Swedish lakes and reservoirs. The Eurasian coot (sothöna) is one of Sweden's most quarrelsome waterbirds: males engage in prolonged and sometimes violent territorial battles during the breeding season, rearing up from the water to kick and slash at rivals with their lobed feet. Coots are supremely adapted divers, using their large, individually lobed toes (rather than webbed feet) to propel themselves underwater in search of aquatic plants, which form the bulk of their diet. Their populations have expanded dramatically on Swedish lowland lakes since the 1970s, in part because nutrient-enriched waters support the dense aquatic vegetation they prefer.

children.name: Fulica atra
children.description: The sothöna — one of the most abundant and aggressive waterbirds in Sweden. Its all-black plumage with brilliant white bill and shield is distinctive at distance. Highly territorial; large lakes may host dozens of pairs separated by invisible but fiercely defended boundaries. Forms large winter flocks on unfrozen coastal bays and deep inland lakes. Nests of the sothöna — built from aquatic plants and anchored in shallow water — are among the most substantial of any Swedish bird.
